name: Donegal Castle
description: A tower house built by the O'Donnell chieftains around 1474, later remodelled with a Jacobean manor wing. It stands at the centre of Donegal town beside the River Eske.
history: Donegal Castle is a castle situated in the centre of Donegal Town in County Donegal in Ulster, the northern province in Ireland. The castle was the stronghold of the O'Donnell clan, Lords of Tír Conaill and one of the most powerful Gaelic families in Ireland from the 5th to the 16th centuries. For most of the last two centuries, the majority of the buildings lay in ruins but the castle was almost fully restored in the early 1990s. It is now open to the public.

name: Doe Castle
description: A MacSweeney tower house spectacularly sited on a peninsula in Sheephaven Bay. Surrounded on three sides by water and a rock-cut moat, it saw many battles over the centuries.
history: Doe Castle, or Caisleán na dTuath, near Creeslough, County Donegal, was the historical stronghold of Clan tSuibhne, with architectural parallels to the Scottish tower house. Built in the early 15th century, it is one of the better fortalices in the north-west of Ireland. The castle sits on a small peninsula, surrounded on three sides by water, with a moat cut into the rock of the landward side. The structure consists mainly of high outer walls around an interior bawn with a four-storey tower-house or keep.

name: Glenveagh Castle
description: A Scottish baronial-style castle on the shores of Lough Veagh in Glenveagh National Park. Built by John George Adair in the 1870s, its gardens are among the finest in Ireland.
history: Glenveagh Castle is a large castellated mansion located in Glenveagh National Park, County Donegal, Ireland and was built in about 1870.

name: Grianan of Aileach
description: An Iron Age stone fort atop Greenan Mountain with panoramic views over Lough Foyle and Lough Swilly. Seat of the O'Neill kings of Aileach, restored in the 1870s by Dr Walter Bernard.
history: The Grianán of Aileach, sometimes anglicised as Greenan Ely or, locally, as Greenan Fort, is a hillfort atop the 244 metres (801 ft) high Greenan Mountain at Burt on the Inishowen Peninsula in County Donegal, Ireland. The main structure is a 19th-century reconstruction of a stone ringfort, thought to have been built by the Northern Uí Néill, in the sixth or seventh century CE; although there is evidence that the site had been in use before the fort was built. It has been identified as the seat of the Kingdom of Ailech and one of the royal sites of Gaelic Ireland. The wall is about 4.5 metres (15 ft) thick and 5 metres (16 ft) high.

name: Boyle Abbey
description: A Cistercian abbey founded in 1161 showing a remarkable transition from Romanesque to Gothic architecture. The contrasting arcade styles on each side of the nave make it architecturally unique.
history: Boyle Abbey is a ruined Cistercian friary located in Boyle, County Roscommon, Ireland. It was founded by Saint Malachy in the year 1161 but not consecrated until 1218.

name: Roscommon Castle
description: A large Norman castle built by Robert d'Ufford in 1269, featuring massive D-shaped corner towers. It changed hands repeatedly between the Irish and English before Cromwell's forces destroyed it.
history: Roscommon is the county town and the largest town in County Roscommon in Ireland. It is roughly in the centre of Ireland, near the meeting of the N60, N61 and N63 roads. The town is in a civil parish of the same name.

name: Ballintober Castle
description: A mighty Anglo-Norman stronghold in County Roscommon, one of the few Irish castles to have been continuously occupied for over 700 years. Its twin-towered gatehouse and massive curtain walls still impress across the flat Connacht landscape.
history: Built c.1300 by the de Burgh family as part of the Norman consolidation of Connacht. One of the few castles in Ireland to be continuously occupied for over 700 years. Features a large enclosure with round corner towers and an imposing gatehouse.

name: Cloughoughter Castle
description: A circular Norman tower on a small island in Lough Oughter, accessible only by boat. Built by the Anglo-Normans on a crannog site, it was the last castle to surrender in the Cromwellian wars.
history: Cloughoughter Castle is a ruined circular castle on a small island in Lough Oughter, 4 kilometres (2.5 mi) east of the town of Killeshandra in County Cavan, Ireland.

name: Cavan Castle
description: Scant but evocative remains of the O'Reilly clan's seat of power, a once-mighty fortress that anchored Gaelic authority in the drumlin heartlands of Ulster.
history: Originally built by the O'Reilly clan in the medieval period, Cavan Castle served as the chief stronghold of the Lords of East Bréifne. The castle was rebuilt and fortified multiple times, playing a role in the Nine Years' War. It was largely destroyed in the Cromwellian conquest and little remains above ground today, the site now absorbed into the town of Cavan.

name: Burt Castle
description: A striking O'Doherty fortress commanding views over Lough Swilly and the Inishowen Peninsula from its dramatic Donegal ridge.
history: Built in the early 16th century by the O'Doherty clan, Burt Castle guards a strategic position overlooking Lough Swilly. It served as the chief O'Doherty residence before being supplanted by nearby Grianan of Aileach. Burned during the O'Doherty rebellion of 1608 and subsequently fell into ruin.

name: Raphoe Castle
description: The fortified palace of the Bishops of Raphoe—a massive, moody ruin in the Donegal midlands where ecclesiastical ambition met military necessity.
history: Built in 1636 by Bishop John Leslie, Raphoe Castle served as the fortified palace of the Church of Ireland bishops of Raphoe. The impressive structure features corner towers and substantial walls. It was damaged during the 1641 Rebellion and Williamite Wars, and abandoned as a residence in the late 18th century.

name: Clogh Oughter Castle
description: A hauntingly beautiful circular tower rising from a tiny island in Lough Oughter, its reflection shimmering in the still waters of Cavan's lake country.
history: Built in the 13th century by the Anglo-Normans on a crannog in Lough Oughter, this unusual circular castle was soon seized by the O'Reilly clan. It held Bishop Bedell during the 1641 Rebellion. The last castle in Ireland to fall to Cromwell's forces in 1653, it was deliberately destroyed by explosion but its walls still stand in the lake.

name: Lough Key Castle
description: The romantic ruin of a MacDermot stronghold on an island in Lough Key, visible from one of Ireland's most beautiful forest parks.
history: Castle Island in Lough Key was the ancient seat of the MacDermot clan, Kings of Moylurg in County Roscommon. The island fortress, known as The Rock, was their stronghold for centuries. The castle and island are now part of Lough Key Forest Park, one of Ireland's premier recreational areas, and can be viewed from the park's treetop walkway.

name: Rindoon Castle
description: A vast, forgotten Anglo-Norman walled town on the shores of Lough Ree, its extensive ruins hidden on a remote peninsula accessible only on foot.
history: Founded in the 1220s during the Anglo-Norman push into Connacht, Rindoon was a fortified town with castle, church, hospital, windmill, and town walls on a peninsula in Lough Ree. It was repeatedly attacked by the Gaelic Irish and abandoned by the 14th century. The extensive ruins spread across the peninsula make it one of Ireland's most atmospheric and least-visited medieval sites.

name: Mongavlin Castle
description: Scant remains of a great O'Donnell stronghold in the Finn Valley, once a centre of Tyrconnell power before the Flight of the Earls changed Ireland forever.
history: Mongavlin Castle near Lifford in County Donegal was a major stronghold of the O'Donnell clan, Lords of Tyrconnell. The castle played a significant role in the Nine Years' War and the events leading to the Flight of the Earls in 1607. Little remains above ground, but the site's historical significance in the story of Gaelic Ireland's final stand is immense.

name: Kilbarron Castle
description: A dramatic clifftop ruin overlooking Donegal Bay, associated with the great Franciscan historian Brother Michael O'Clery and the Annals of the Four Masters.
history: Kilbarron Castle near Ballyshannon was the ancestral home of the O'Clery family, hereditary historians to the O'Donnells. Brother Michael O'Clery, chief compiler of the Annals of the Four Masters (one of Ireland's greatest historical manuscripts), was born here around 1590. The castle ruins perch dramatically on cliffs above Donegal Bay.

name: Castle Murray
description: A ruined tower on the dramatic Donegal coast near Dunkineely, overlooking the wild Atlantic waters of McSwyne's Bay.
history: Castle Murray, also known as McSwyne's Castle, stands on the coast near Dunkineely in County Donegal. It was associated with the MacSweeney clan, gallowglass warriors who served the O'Donnells. The castle commands views over McSwyne's Bay and the Atlantic. A well-known restaurant and hotel nearby take the castle's name.

name: Malin Castle
description: Ruins of a 17th-century plantation house near Ireland's most northerly point, where the wild Inishowen Peninsula meets the Atlantic.
history: Malin Castle, near the village of Malin in Inishowen, County Donegal, dates to the early 17th century plantation period. The ruins stand near Malin Head, Ireland's most northerly point. The building served as a fortified plantation house during the Ulster Plantation. Malin Head was later used as a weather reporting station and wartime lookout.

name: Carrickabraghy Castle
description: A remote and dramatic tower house on the Isle of Doagh, Inishowen, battered by Atlantic storms at the very edge of the inhabited world.
history: Carrickabraghy Castle on the Isle of Doagh, Inishowen Peninsula, was built in the 16th century, possibly by the O'Doherty clan. The castle stands on a rocky promontory exposed to the full force of the Atlantic. Its remote and dramatic setting at the northernmost tip of Inishowen makes it one of Ireland's most wildly situated castles.

name: Rathmullan Priory
description: Evocative ruins of a Carmelite friary on the shores of Lough Swilly, where the Flight of the Earls departed Ireland forever in 1607.
history: Rathmullan Priory was founded by the Carmelite order in the 15th century on the western shore of Lough Swilly, County Donegal. The site gained its greatest historical significance on 14 September 1607, when the Earls of Tyrone and Tyrconnell sailed from Rathmullan in the Flight of the Earls, ending the old Gaelic order in Ireland. The friary ruins are well-preserved with fine carved details.

name: Castlerea Castle
description: Modest castle remains in the Roscommon town that shares its name, where the story of the Gaelic west meets the reality of English plantation.
history: Castlerea in County Roscommon takes its name from its medieval castle. The town later became associated with the Sandford family and their Clonalis House estate nearby, seat of the O'Conor Don, descendants of the last High Kings of Ireland. The castle remains are fragmentary but the town's heritage is rich with Gaelic and plantation history.

name: Donamon Castle
description: A fine medieval tower house beside the River Suck, now home to a Divine Word Missionaries religious community in the quiet Roscommon countryside.
history: Donamon Castle on the River Suck in County Roscommon dates to the 13th century and was held by the Kings of Connacht. The castle features a medieval tower house with later additions. In the 20th century, it was acquired by the Divine Word Missionaries who have used it as a retreat centre. The castle and grounds are set in a beautiful riverside location.

name: Cabra Castle
description: A romantic 18th-century castle hotel draped in ivy and legend — where the drumlands of Cavan meet centuries of Irish intrigue and old-world elegance.
history: Dating back to 1760, built on land that originally contained the medieval Cormey Castle. The site changed hands during the Cromwellian Wars. The current castle was built by the Foster family and later expanded in the Gothic style in 1813. The aristocratic Pratt family lived nearby on the Cabra Estate. Now operates as a luxury castle hotel surrounded by ancient parkland.

name: Castle Saunderson
description: A once-grand Big House on the Cavan-Fermanagh border, now the International Scout Centre of Ireland, where history meets outdoor adventure.
history: Castle Saunderson near Belturbet, County Cavan, was the seat of the Saunderson family from the 17th century. Colonel Edward Saunderson was a prominent Unionist politician. The castle was burned in 1922 and later partly rebuilt. In 2012, the estate was developed as Scouting Ireland's International Scout Centre, offering camping, kayaking, and outdoor activities on the shores of the River Erne.

name: Castle Leslie
description: A Scottish baronial fantasy set on a thousand wild acres in County Monaghan — where Jonathan Swift once stayed and the Leslies have held court since 1665.
history: Built in 1870-71 in Scottish baronial style by architect W.H. Lynn for Sir John Leslie, 1st Baronet, on the site of a 17th-century mansion. The Leslie family acquired the estate in 1665 when 'fighting bishop' John Leslie bought it with funds from Charles II. Features a Renaissance cloister inspired by Michelangelo's design in Rome. Now operates as a luxury hotel.

name: Mannan Castle
description: A medieval motte and later tower near Carrickmacross, guarding the ancient route through the drumlin country of south Monaghan.
history: Mannan Castle near Carrickmacross, County Monaghan, features a Norman motte with later medieval additions. The site controlled a route through the drumlin landscape of south Ulster. The area was part of the McMahon territory. The castle site is now part of a golf course but retains historical interest as a marker of Norman expansion into Ulster.

name: Castle Balfour
description: A substantial Scottish planter's castle in the Fermanagh town of Lisnaskea, one of the best-preserved plantation castles in Ulster.
history: Built around 1618 by Captain James Balfour, a Scottish planter, Castle Balfour in Lisnaskea is one of the finest surviving examples of a plantation castle in Ulster. The T-shaped building features Scottish architectural details including corbelled turrets. It was damaged during the 1641 Rebellion and Williamite Wars but substantial ruins remain.

name: Castle Archdale
description: A plantation castle estate on the shores of Lower Lough Erne, famous as a World War II flying boat base and now a popular country park.
history: Castle Archdale on the shores of Lower Lough Erne was built by the Archdale family, English planters who arrived in the 17th century. During World War II, the estate served as a base for Catalina and Sunderland flying boats that patrolled the Atlantic for U-boats. The estate is now a popular country park with camping, cycling, and boat trips to White Island's mysterious carved figures.

name: Mountjoy Castle
description: A ruined Elizabethan fort on the shores of Lough Neagh, built during the Nine Years' War as a springboard for the conquest of Ulster.
history: Built in 1602 by Lord Mountjoy, the English Lord Deputy, as part of his campaign against Hugh O'Neill during the Nine Years' War. Mountjoy Castle near Coalisland, County Tyrone, stands on the shores of Lough Neagh. The fort was a strategic military installation that helped secure English control of Ulster. It was garrisoned until the mid-17th century and then fell into disuse.

name: Newtownstewart Castle
description: A massive plantation castle ruin dominating the Tyrone village of Newtownstewart, its shattered walls a monument to the turbulent Ulster Plantation.
history: Newtownstewart Castle in County Tyrone was built in the early 17th century as part of the Ulster Plantation. The substantial castle was destroyed by fire during the Williamite Wars in 1689 when the retreating Jacobite forces burned it. The impressive ruins, with their massive walls and window openings, stand prominently in the village centre.

name: Castlecaulfield
description: Elegant ruins of a Jacobean mansion in County Tyrone, where Renaissance refinement met the raw frontier of plantation Ulster.
history: Built around 1619 by Sir Toby Caulfield on the site of an O'Donnelly fort, Castlecaulfield is one of the finest examples of a Jacobean plantation house in Ireland. The mansion featured large mullioned windows, ornamental gatehouse, and elegant proportions. Burned by Patrick O'Donnelly during the 1641 Rebellion, the atmospheric ruins display the confident ambition of the planter class.

name: Brackfield Bawn
description: A rare surviving plantation bawn in County Derry, its defensive walls a tangible reminder of the insecure world of Ulster's settler community.
history: Brackfield Bawn near Claudy in County Derry is a rare surviving example of a plantation-era fortified enclosure (bawn). Built in the early 17th century as part of the Plantation of Ulster, the bawn provided a defensive compound for settler families. These enclosures were a distinctive feature of the plantation landscape, and Brackfield's survival makes it an important heritage site.

name: Navan Fort
description: The legendary seat of Ulster's ancient kings — a mystical hilltop where a massive timber temple was ritually burned and buried 2,000 years ago, pulsing with the mythology of Cu Chulainn and the Red Branch Knights.
history: One of Ireland's great royal sites, traditionally the capital of the Ulaidh. Archaeological excavation revealed a huge timber structure that was filled with stones, deliberately burned, and covered with earth in a ritual act around 95 BC. Features prominently in the Ulster Cycle of Irish mythology. Part of the larger Navan complex including Haughey's Fort and the King's Stables ritual pool.

name: Strangford Castle
description: A compact tower house guarding the narrow entrance to Strangford Lough, one of Europe's great sea loughs and a wildlife wonderland.
history: Strangford Castle is a 16th-century tower house in the village of Strangford, County Down, overlooking the narrow strait that connects Strangford Lough to the Irish Sea. The name 'Strangford' comes from the Old Norse 'Strangr Fjorthr' meaning strong inlet, reflecting the powerful tidal currents. The castle, along with nearby Portaferry Castle, guarded this strategically vital waterway.

name: Ardglass Castles
description: A remarkable cluster of medieval tower houses and fortifications guarding an ancient Down harbour, the most castle-dense village in Ireland.
history: Ardglass in County Down boasts the greatest concentration of medieval castles in any village in Ireland. Jordan's Castle (15th century) is the best preserved and now houses a small museum. Other castles include King's Castle, Isabella's Tower, Cowd Castle, and Margaret's Castle. The town was one of the busiest ports in Ulster during the medieval period, hence its impressive fortifications.

name: Portaferry Castle
description: A sturdy 16th-century tower house guarding the eastern shore of the Strangford Lough narrows, facing its twin at Strangford across the rushing tide.
history: Portaferry Castle is a 16th-century tower house on the eastern shore of the Strangford Lough narrows in County Down. Built by the Savage family, it controlled the eastern approach to the lough alongside Strangford Castle on the opposite shore. The castle has been restored and houses a visitor information centre.

name: Mahee Island Nendrum
description: An early Christian monastery on a tidal island in Strangford Lough, where concentric stone walls enclose one of Ireland's most complete monastic sites.
history: Nendrum Monastery on Mahee Island in Strangford Lough was founded by St Mochaoi in the 5th century. The site features three concentric enclosures, a round tower stump, church ruins, and a remarkably well-preserved sundial—one of the oldest in Ireland. Excavations revealed a monastic tide mill dating to 619 AD, one of the earliest known in the world.

name: Castlestrange Stone
description: A remarkable La Tene-period decorated stone in the Roscommon countryside—one of only three such carved stones in Ireland, covered in mysterious Iron Age spirals.
history: The Castlestrange Stone near Athleague, County Roscommon, is a rounded granite boulder decorated with flowing La Tene (Celtic Iron Age) spiral carvings dating to around 500 BC-100 AD. It is one of only three such decorated stones known in Ireland (with Turoe Stone and Killycluggin). The purpose of these stones remains debated—possibly ceremonial or marking a sacred site.

name: Fuerty Castle
description: Medieval remains near the village of Fuerty in Roscommon, in an area rich with ecclesiastical heritage dating back to the early Christian period.
history: Fuerty Castle is a medieval fortification near the village of Fuerty, County Roscommon. The village has ancient ecclesiastical connections, with a monastery associated with St Justinian. The castle dates to the medieval period and was held by local Roscommon families.

name: Tulsk Castle
description: Castle remains in the historically rich village of Tulsk, gateway to the legendary Rathcroghan complex—ancient capital of the Kings of Connacht.
history: Tulsk Castle in County Roscommon stands in a village of immense historical significance. Nearby Rathcroghan (Cruachan) was the ancient capital of the Kings of Connacht and the traditional entrance to the Celtic Otherworld. The castle dates to the medieval period and a Dominican priory was also founded here. An interpretive centre explains the area's rich archaeological heritage.

name: Dunamon Tower
description: A medieval tower on the River Suck in County Roscommon, now part of a Divine Word Missionaries community centre.
history: Dunamon Tower is a medieval fortification on the River Suck in County Roscommon. The castle dates to the medieval period and was held by the Kings of Connacht. Later acquired by the Divine Word Missionaries, the tower and surrounding buildings serve as a religious community centre in a beautiful riverside setting.

name: Ballyfarnon Castle
description: A quiet ruin in the Roscommon village of Ballyfarnon, near the shores of Lough Key in one of Ireland's most beautiful lake districts.
history: Ballyfarnon Castle is a medieval fortification near Ballyfarnon, County Roscommon. The village sits near Lough Key, one of Ireland's most scenic lakes, now the centrepiece of Lough Key Forest Park. The castle dates to the medieval period and was associated with local Roscommon families.

name: Ballynakill Castle
description: A tower house in the Roscommon countryside, one of the chain of medieval fortifications that controlled the fertile midlands.
history: Ballynakill Castle is a medieval tower house in County Roscommon. Built in the 15th or 16th century, it was part of the network of fortified residences in the Roscommon midlands. The tower served as a local stronghold for one of the region's landowning families.

name: Castleplunket Tower
description: A small medieval tower near the Roscommon village of Castleplunket, associated with the Plunkett family who held lands across the Irish midlands.
history: Castleplunket Tower in County Roscommon is a medieval fortification associated with the Plunkett family. The Plunketts were a significant Anglo-Norman family with extensive holdings across the Irish midlands. The tower dates to the medieval period and gave its name to the surrounding village.

name: Kilronan Castle
description: A magnificently restored 18th-century castle now operating as a luxury hotel, set in the rolling drumlin landscape of County Roscommon.
history: Kilronan Castle near Ballyfarnon was originally built in the 18th century by the Tenison family. After falling into disrepair, the castle was extensively restored and reopened as a luxury hotel in 2006. The estate sits in beautiful parkland near Lough Meelagh and offers a blend of period grandeur and modern comfort.

name: Glinsk Castle
description: A striking fortified house on the Galway-Roscommon border, its ornate facade an unusually refined piece of 17th-century architecture for the Irish west.
history: Glinsk Castle on the Galway-Roscommon border was built around 1630 by the Burke family. The castle is notable for its ornate facade with carved figures and decorative stonework—unusual sophistication for a building on the western frontier. The castle was confiscated during the Cromwellian settlement and fell into disrepair.

name: Inch Castle
description: A dramatic O'Doherty tower house on a peninsula jutting into Lough Swilly, one of Donegal's most photogenic castle ruins.
history: Inch Castle on the shores of Lough Swilly, County Donegal, was an O'Doherty stronghold. The castle stands on Inch Island, connected to the mainland by a causeway. It was burned during the O'Doherty Rebellion of 1608 and never repaired. The atmospheric ruins overlooking Lough Swilly make it one of Donegal's most photographed heritage sites.

name: Buncrana Castle
description: O'Doherty castle ruins in the Inishowen resort town of Buncrana, where the shores of Lough Swilly meet the hills of Donegal's largest peninsula.
history: Buncrana Castle (also known as O'Doherty's Keep) stands in the town of Buncrana on the shores of Lough Swilly, County Donegal. Built by the O'Doherty clan, the castle dates to the medieval period. Nearby is the later Buncrana Castle built by John Vaughan in 1718, where Wolfe Tone was briefly held after his capture in 1798.

name: Moross Castle
description: A tower house ruin in County Donegal, standing in the wild landscape of the northwest where mountains meet the Atlantic.
history: Moross Castle is a medieval tower house in County Donegal. The castle dates to the medieval period and was associated with local chieftain families. The Donegal landscape of mountains, lakes, and Atlantic coast provides a dramatic setting for its many historical sites.

name: Drumboe Castle
description: A castellated mansion near Stranorlar in Donegal, with a tragic Civil War history when four anti-Treaty prisoners were executed here in 1923.
history: Drumboe Castle near Stranorlar, County Donegal, is a 19th-century castellated mansion. During the Irish Civil War, four anti-Treaty IRA prisoners were executed by firing squad in the castle grounds in March 1923, a controversial act that shocked the local community. A memorial marks the site. The castle later served various purposes.

name: Na Dúnaibh Castle
description: Tower house near Downings on the Rosguill Peninsula.

name: Assaroe Abbey
description: Fragmentary Cistercian abbey ruins near Ballyshannon, beside the falls where the River Erne once tumbled into Donegal Bay.
history: Assaroe Abbey near Ballyshannon, County Donegal, was a Cistercian monastery founded in the 12th century. The abbey sits near where the River Erne's waterfalls (now harnessed for hydroelectric power) once provided one of Ireland's most spectacular natural scenes. The abbey ruins are fragmentary but the site retains historical significance in this ancient crossing point.

name: Castlemore
description: An Anglo-Norman motte and castle ruin in County Carlow, one of the early fortifications built during the Norman conquest of Leinster.
history: Castlemore in County Carlow features a Norman motte and the remains of a medieval castle. The motte was constructed during the early phase of Norman expansion into Leinster in the late 12th century. The site was strategically positioned to control routes through the Barrow Valley.

name: Movilla Abbey
description: One of Ireland's most important early Christian monastic sites, founded in the 6th century in the County Down town of Newtownards.
history: Movilla Abbey in Newtownards, County Down, was founded by St Finian in the 6th century. It became one of the most important monastic schools in Ireland and possibly produced the Movilla Psalter. The Augustinian priory ruins date to the medieval period. The site is within a graveyard and retains its ancient spiritual atmosphere.

name: Shane Castle
description: The O'Neill seat on the shores of Lough Neagh—a grand estate where Ireland's most powerful Gaelic dynasty held court.
history: Shane's Castle on the shores of Lough Neagh, County Antrim, has been the seat of the O'Neill family since the 16th century. The castle was badly damaged by fire in 1816 and never fully rebuilt. The estate includes extensive grounds with a narrow-gauge railway, nature reserve, and ruins. The O'Neills were the most powerful dynasty in Ulster for centuries.

name: Harry Avery Castle
description: An unusual hilltop castle in County Tyrone with twin D-shaped towers, named after a 14th-century O'Neill chieftain—a rare Gaelic stone castle.
history: Harry Avery's Castle near Newtownstewart, County Tyrone, is one of the few stone castles built by a Gaelic Irish chieftain. Named after Henry Aimhreidh O'Neill, a 14th-century lord, the castle features unusual twin D-shaped towers flanking the entrance. The hilltop position provides commanding views across the Tyrone countryside. It is a rare example of Gaelic lords adopting Norman-style stone fortification.

name: Castle Coole
description: Ireland's finest neoclassical mansion — a Palladian masterpiece in Portland stone where even the servants' entrance was hidden underground to preserve the perfect facade.
history: Built 1789-1797 for the 1st Earl of Belmore, designed by James Wyatt in severe neoclassical style. The Portland stone was shipped from Dorset and carried overland by bullock cart. Features magnificent Regency interiors including a state bedroom prepared for George IV (who never visited). Remarkably, the mansion has no above-ground service door. Now a National Trust property.

name: Florence Court
description: An elegant Georgian mansion set against the wild foothills of Cuilcagh Mountain — home of the Cole family for 250 years and parent of every Irish yew tree in the world.
history: Built c.1720 by Sir John Cole, named after his wife Florence Bourchier Wrey. The grand Palladian wings were added by the 1st Earl of Enniskillen in the 1770s. The Cole family (Earls of Enniskillen) lived here for over 250 years. Badly damaged by fire in 1955, just one year after transfer to the National Trust, but restored using comprehensive records. The famous Florence Court Yew in the gardens is reputedly the ancestor of all Irish yew trees.

name: Rathcroghan
description: The legendary capital of ancient Connacht and entrance to the Celtic Otherworld—one of the most important archaeological complexes in Europe.
history: Rathcroghan (Cruachan Ai) near Tulsk, County Roscommon, was the ancient capital of the Kings of Connacht and features in the great Irish epic Tain Bo Cuailnge. The complex includes over 60 monuments including a royal inauguration mound, ringforts, and Oweynagat ('Cave of the Cats')—traditionally the entrance to the Celtic Otherworld. A visitor centre at Tulsk interprets this extraordinary landscape.

name: Castle Ward
description: A unique National Trust estate with two architectural personalities—Classical on one side, Gothic on the other—reflecting a husband and wife who couldn't agree on style.
history: Castle Ward near Strangford, County Down, is a unique 18th-century mansion with its front in Classical Palladian style (Lord Bangor's choice) and its rear in Gothic (Lady Bangor's preference). The estate overlooks Strangford Lough and has been used as a filming location for Game of Thrones (as Winterfell). Now a National Trust property with extensive grounds and activities.

name: Donegal Abbey
description: Franciscan friary ruins on the banks of Donegal Bay, where the Annals of the Four Masters—Ireland's greatest historical chronicle—were compiled.
history: Donegal Abbey (Donegal Friary) was a Franciscan foundation established in 1474 by Red Hugh O'Donnell and his wife Nuala O'Brien. It is most famous as the place where the Annals of the Four Masters were compiled (1632-36), recording Irish history from the Deluge to 1616. The atmospheric ruins stand on the shore of Donegal Bay in the town centre.

name: Strokestown House
description: A magnificent Palladian mansion housing the National Famine Museum, where harrowing landlord correspondence reveals the human tragedy of Ireland's darkest chapter.
history: Strokestown Park House in County Roscommon is a Palladian mansion built in the 1730s. The house is most significant for its National Famine Museum, which uses the estate's archive of landlord-tenant correspondence to tell the devastating story of the Great Famine (1845-1852). Major Denis Mahon, the landlord, was assassinated in 1847. The house, museum, and restored gardens form one of Ireland's most important heritage sites.

name: Drumlane Abbey
description: Well-preserved medieval monastic ruins near Milltown in County Cavan, featuring a fine round tower stump and Augustinian church.
history: Drumlane Abbey near Milltown, County Cavan, combines a round tower stump with substantial Augustinian church ruins. The monastic site dates to the early Christian period, with the surviving buildings primarily medieval. The round tower and church are set in a quiet, atmospheric graveyard in the Cavan drumlin country.

name: Kilmore Cathedral
description: The cathedral of the Diocese of Kilmore in County Cavan, incorporating a remarkable Romanesque doorway rescued from a medieval monastery on a nearby island.
history: Kilmore Cathedral in County Cavan incorporates a magnificent 12th-century Romanesque doorway that was originally part of a monastery on Trinity Island in Lough Oughter. The richly carved doorway features interlaced patterns and human heads. The current cathedral dates to 1860 but the Romanesque doorway makes it a significant medieval heritage site.

name: Monaghan Castle
description: Fragmentary remains of a plantation-era castle in the county town of Monaghan, where the complexities of Ulster's colonial history are writ in stone.
history: Monaghan Castle was built in the early 17th century as part of the plantation of Ulster. The castle served as an English military and administrative centre. Little survives above ground, but the site is historically significant as a marker of the plantation's impact on this part of Ulster. The town of Monaghan developed around the castle.

name: Clones Round Tower
description: A fine round tower in the Monaghan border town of Clones, where early Christian heritage meets the complex history of the Ulster borderlands.
history: The Round Tower at Clones, County Monaghan, dates to the 10th-12th century and is part of an early Christian monastic complex that also includes a high cross and a sarcophagus in the Diamond (town square). Clones was an important monastic centre founded by St Tiernach in the 6th century. The town later became famous as a GAA stronghold and lace-making centre.

name: Mussenden Temple
description: A tiny classical temple teetering on a 120-foot cliff above the Atlantic — built as a bishop's library and modelled on the Temple of Vesta in Tivoli, with Game of Thrones coastline stretching below.
history: Built in 1785 by Frederick Hervey, the eccentric Earl-Bishop of Derry, as a library within the Downhill Demesne. Modelled on the Temple of Vesta at Tivoli, Italy. Named after his cousin Frideswide Mussenden, who died aged 22 before its completion. Cliff erosion has brought the temple perilously close to the edge. The nearby Downhill House is now a ruin. Managed by the National Trust.

name: Downhill House
description: The spectacular roofless shell of an 18th-century bishop's palace perched on the wild Derry clifftops, with the iconic Mussenden Temple clinging to the cliff edge.
history: Downhill House was built in the 1770s for Frederick Hervey, the eccentric Earl Bishop of Derry. The grand house was damaged by fire twice and finally abandoned. Its roofless shell stands dramatically on the Antrim coast cliffs. The nearby Mussenden Temple, inspired by the Temple of Vesta at Tivoli, perches precariously on the cliff edge overlooking the Atlantic.

name: Sampson's Tower Derry
description: A historic tower within the walls of Derry, part of the finest example of a walled city in Ireland and Britain.
history: Sampson's Tower is one of the features of Derry's city walls, the most complete and finest example of a walled city in Ireland and Britain. The walls were built between 1613 and 1619 during the Plantation of Ulster and have never been breached. They played a crucial role during the famous Siege of Derry in 1689.

name: Armagh Friary
description: Franciscan friary ruins in the ancient ecclesiastical capital of Ireland, where St Patrick established his church in the 5th century.
history: Armagh Friary is part of the rich heritage of Armagh, Ireland's ecclesiastical capital since St Patrick established his church here in 445 AD. The city is the seat of both the Catholic and Church of Ireland Archbishops of Armagh. The friary ruins add to the medieval religious heritage of this historically significant city.

name: Kilnasaggart Pillar Stone
description: One of the oldest dated Christian monuments in Ireland, an 8th-century inscribed pillar stone in the Armagh countryside.
history: The Kilnasaggart Pillar Stone near Jonesborough, County Armagh, dates to around 700 AD and is one of the earliest inscribed Christian monuments in Ireland. The inscription records a grant of the site to a person named Ternoc. The stone is decorated with crosses and the inscription is in Irish. It stands at an ancient crossroads in a simple enclosure.

name: Clonalis House
description: The ancestral home of the O'Conor Don—descendants of the last High Kings of Ireland—housing a treasure trove of manuscripts and the inaugural harp of the kings.
history: Clonalis House near Castlerea, County Roscommon, is the seat of the O'Conor Don, direct descendants of the last High Kings of Ireland. The Victorian Italianate mansion houses a remarkable collection including the coronation stone of the Kings of Connacht, the harp of Turlough O'Carolan (Ireland's national composer), and manuscripts dating back 1,500 years.

name: Fuerty Church
description: A medieval church ruin near Roscommon town, in an area of rich ecclesiastical heritage stretching back to early Christianity.
history: Fuerty Church near Roscommon is a medieval church ruin associated with the ancient monastic foundation of St Justinian. The site preserves medieval church architecture in the Roscommon countryside.

name: Aghalurcher Church
description: A medieval church ruin in County Fermanagh, in the quiet lake country of the upper Erne basin.
history: Aghalurcher Church is a medieval church ruin in County Fermanagh. The site has early Christian origins and the medieval church remains stand in a traditional graveyard. The area is part of the lake-studded Erne basin landscape.

name: Lough Navar Castle
description: A viewpoint and fortification site in the Fermanagh lakelands, offering one of Ireland's finest panoramic views across Lower Lough Erne.
history: Lough Navar Forest in County Fermanagh includes castle ruins and offers the famous Cliffs of Magho viewpoint—one of the finest panoramic views in Ireland, stretching across Lower Lough Erne to the Donegal mountains. The forest park contains trails, wildlife habitats, and historical features.

name: Lisnaskea Castle
description: Castle remains in the Fermanagh market town of Lisnaskea, near the shores of Upper Lough Erne in the heart of the Lakeland district.
history: Lisnaskea in County Fermanagh has castle remains near the town centre. The town sits near the shores of Upper Lough Erne and has a long history as a market town. Nearby Castle Balfour is the more substantial castle in the town.

name: Ballinlough Castle Roscommon
description: A fine castle and estate near Clonmellon on the Roscommon-Westmeath border, hosting music festivals in its beautiful parkland.
history: Ballinlough Castle on the Roscommon-Westmeath border is a castle estate that dates to the medieval period. The castle has been home to the same family for centuries and the estate grounds now host festivals and events including the Body & Soul music festival. The parkland and lake setting creates an atmospheric venue.

name: Nendrum Monastic Site
description: See Mahee Island Nendrum—one of the most complete early Christian monastic enclosures in Ireland, on a tidal island in Strangford Lough.
history: Nendrum on Mahee Island in Strangford Lough, County Down, is one of Ireland's best-excavated early monastic sites with three concentric enclosures, church, round tower stump, and a remarkably early tidal mill (dated 619 AD).

name: Castlecaldwell Castle
description: A ruined castle on the shores of Lower Lough Erne, now a forest park famous for its fiddler's stone and birdwatching opportunities.
history: Castlecaldwell on the shores of Lower Lough Erne, County Fermanagh, is a ruined castle now within a forest park. The estate is famous for the Fiddler's Stone, commemorating a musician who drowned after falling off a boat while playing. The area is excellent for birdwatching with common scoter ducks nesting on the lake islands.

name: Errigal Keerogue Cross
description: A fine early Christian carved cross in County Tyrone, preserving intricate patterns from the golden age of Irish art.
history: Errigal Keerogue in County Tyrone preserves a fine early Christian cross and church remains. The cross features intricate carvings characteristic of the golden age of Irish ecclesiastical art (8th-10th centuries). The rural setting in mid-Tyrone adds to the atmospheric quality.

name: Tullaghoge Fort
description: The ancient inauguration site of the O'Neill kings of Tyrone, destroyed by Mountjoy in 1602 to symbolically end Gaelic sovereignty.
history: Tullaghoge Fort near Cookstown, County Tyrone, was the inauguration site of the O'Neill dynasty. Here new chieftains were crowned on a sacred stone chair. In 1602, Lord Mountjoy deliberately destroyed the stone chair to symbolically end O'Neill authority. The hilltop fort remains an evocative site of enormous cultural significance.

name: Caledon Castle
description: A grand estate in County Tyrone, home to the Earl of Caledon, surrounded by parkland and the picturesque village of Caledon.
history: Caledon Castle in County Tyrone is the seat of the Earl of Caledon. The grand Georgian house is set in extensive parkland. The nearby village of Caledon is one of Northern Ireland's prettiest planned estate villages.

name: Donaghmore High Cross
description: A magnificent reconstructed high cross in the Tyrone village of Donaghmore, featuring intricate biblical scenes carved in stone.
history: The High Cross at Donaghmore, County Tyrone, is a fine early Christian cross reconstructed from two fragments. The cross features detailed biblical scenes including the Fall of Adam and Eve. Donaghmore also preserves the remains of a monastery founded by St Patrick.

name: Ardboe High Cross
description: One of the finest high crosses in Ulster, standing 5.5 metres tall on the western shore of Lough Neagh with 22 carved biblical panels.
history: The Ardboe High Cross on the shores of Lough Neagh, County Tyrone, is one of the best-preserved high crosses in Northern Ireland. Standing 5.5 metres tall, it features 22 carved panels depicting Old and New Testament scenes. The cross dates to the 10th century and stands beside an early Christian church site.

name: Gosford Castle
description: A spectacular Norman Revival castle in County Armagh, designed by Thomas Hopper—one of the largest and most dramatic 19th-century castles in Ireland.
history: Gosford Castle near Markethill, County Armagh, was built between 1819 and 1850 by Thomas Hopper for the Acheson family (Earls of Gosford) in a dramatic Norman Revival style. The massive castle, one of the largest 19th-century castles in Ireland, was used as a military base during WWII. It has been converted into apartments in recent years.

name: Ballymoyer Church
description: A medieval church ruin in the Armagh countryside, in the rolling drumlin landscape of south Armagh.
history: Ballymoyer Church is a medieval church ruin in County Armagh, in the quiet rural landscape of south Armagh.

name: Seaforde Butterfly House
description: A tropical butterfly house and maze in the grounds of a historic County Down estate, combining natural history with heritage gardens.
history: Seaforde in County Down is home to the Seaforde Butterfly House and Tropical Gardens, set in the grounds of the historic Seaforde estate. The gardens feature a hornbeam maze and tropical greenhouse with free-flying butterflies.

name: Ballycopeland Windmill
description: The only surviving complete working windmill in Ireland, beautifully preserved near the County Down coast.
history: Ballycopeland Windmill near Millisle, County Down, is the only surviving complete working windmill in Ireland. Built around 1790, the windmill operated until 1915. It has been restored to working order and is open to visitors.

name: Tory Island Round Tower
description: A round tower on Ireland's most remote inhabited island, where an ancient Celtic-speaking community maintains traditions stretching back millennia.
history: Tory Island off the Donegal coast preserves a round tower and early Christian monastic remains. The island has been continuously inhabited for millennia and maintains a unique culture including its own 'king.' The island is also famous for its school of naive painters, founded in the 1950s.

name: Carrigart Castle
description: Castle remains in the Donegal coastal village of Carrigart, gateway to the wild Rosguill Peninsula and the Atlantic Drive.
history: Carrigart Castle in County Donegal stands in the village that serves as gateway to the Rosguill Peninsula and its famous Atlantic Drive, one of Ireland's most spectacular coastal roads.

name: Tullydonnell Castle
description: A tower house ruin in the Inishowen Peninsula, County Donegal, in Ireland's most northerly landscape.
history: Tullydonnell Castle on the Inishowen Peninsula, County Donegal, is a medieval tower house in Ireland's most northerly region.

name: Clonca Church
description: A medieval church in Inishowen, Donegal, featuring a remarkable carved tombstone with a sword, hurley, and ball—the earliest known depiction of hurling.
history: Clonca Church in Inishowen, County Donegal, preserves a remarkable medieval tombstone featuring a carved sword, hurley stick, and ball—believed to be the earliest known depiction of the Irish sport of hurling. The church also features a fine carved lintel and high cross.

name: Cruachan Ai Visitor Centre
description: An interpretive centre at Tulsk explaining the extraordinary Rathcroghan archaeological complex—ancient capital of Connacht's kings.
history: The Cruachan Ai Visitor Centre at Tulsk, County Roscommon, interprets the remarkable Rathcroghan archaeological complex nearby, including the royal mound, ringforts, and Oweynagat cave—the legendary entrance to the Celtic Otherworld.

name: Cloonfree Castle
description: A tower house in the Roscommon countryside, one of the scattered medieval fortifications in the Irish midlands.
history: Cloonfree Castle is a medieval tower house in County Roscommon.

name: Cavan Burren Park
description: A remarkable megalithic landscape in County Cavan, featuring wedge tombs, standing stones, and geological formations in a scenic forest park.
history: Cavan Burren Park near Blacklion, County Cavan, is a remarkable landscape of megalithic tombs, standing stones, and natural geological features in a forest setting. The park features a network of trails leading to wedge tombs dating to the Bronze Age and other archaeological monuments.

name: Ballyjamesduff Castle
description: Castle remains in the Cavan market town of Ballyjamesduff, named after James Duff who received the lands in the 17th century.
history: Ballyjamesduff in County Cavan has castle remains. The town is named after Captain James Duff, who received lands here during the Cromwellian settlement. The Cavan County Museum in the town holds significant archaeological collections.

name: Ballyhaise House
description: A fine Palladian house in County Cavan, designed by Richard Cassels, now serving as a campus for Dundalk Institute of Technology.
history: Ballyhaise House in County Cavan was designed by the architect Richard Cassels (Castle) around 1733. The fine Palladian house now serves as a campus for DKIT (Dundalk Institute of Technology) agricultural college.

name: Hope Castle Castleblayney
description: A castellated mansion in the Monaghan town of Castleblayney, set in the beautiful Lough Muckno parkland.
history: Hope Castle in Castleblayney, County Monaghan, was the seat of the Blayney and Hope families. The castellated mansion overlooks Lough Muckno and its parkland is now a popular recreational area with walking trails and fishing.

name: Dartrey Estate
description: A ruined mansion in the Monaghan countryside, set in extensive demesne parkland beside the shores of Dartrey Lake.
history: Dartrey Estate near Rockcorry, County Monaghan, was the seat of the Dawson family (Earls of Dartrey). The mansion has fallen into ruin but the extensive parkland and estate landscape remain. The grounds include fine mature woodland and a lake.

name: Inniskeen Round Tower
description: A round tower stump in the Monaghan village of Inniskeen, birthplace of Patrick Kavanagh, one of Ireland's greatest poets.
history: The Round Tower at Inniskeen, County Monaghan, marks an early Christian monastic site. The village is most famous as the birthplace of Patrick Kavanagh (1904-1967), whose poetry celebrating the ordinary beauty of rural Monaghan ('On Raglan Road', 'The Great Hunger') made him one of Ireland's greatest poets. A museum tells his story.

name: Lough Oughter Castle Island
description: See Clogh Oughter Castle—a hauntingly beautiful circular tower on an island in Lough Oughter, the last castle to fall to Cromwell.
history: See Clogh Oughter Castle entry. The circular castle rising from the island in Lough Oughter is one of Ireland's most atmospheric and photogenic ruins.

name: Banagher Old Church Derry
description: A remarkable early Christian church ruin in County Derry with fine stone carvings and a rare medieval tomb shrine.
history: Banagher Old Church near Dungiven, County Derry, is an early Christian church site with fine stone carvings. The nearby Dungiven Priory contains the tomb of Cooey na Gall O'Cahan, one of the finest medieval tomb shrines in Ireland, featuring carved gallowglass warriors.

name: Bovevagh Church
description: A medieval church ruin in the Roe Valley of County Derry, in a beautiful area of woodland, rivers, and historical heritage.
history: Bovevagh Church is a medieval church ruin in the Roe Valley, County Derry. The Roe Valley is a scenic area of woodland and rivers, now largely a country park. The church dates to the medieval period.

name: Springhill House
description: A charming 17th-century plantation house in County Derry, one of the National Trust's most atmospheric properties in Northern Ireland.
history: Springhill House near Moneymore, County Derry, is a 17th-century plantation house built by the Conyngham family. The charming whitewashed house features original furniture, a costume collection, and a reputed ghost. It has been a National Trust property since 1957 and is one of the most atmospheric historic houses in Northern Ireland.

name: Killough Towers
description: Tower remains in the County Down coastal village of Killough, a planned estate town on the shores of the Irish Sea.
history: Killough in County Down is a charming planned estate village with tower remains and a small harbour. The village was developed as a port by the Ward family of Castle Ward in the 18th century.

name: Struell Wells
description: A remarkable group of medieval bath houses fed by holy wells near Downpatrick, associated with St Patrick and ancient healing traditions.
history: Struell Wells near Downpatrick, County Down, is a group of medieval bath houses fed by holy wells. Associated with St Patrick, the wells were famous for their healing properties and attracted pilgrims for centuries. The site includes drinking well, eye well, and separate bath houses for men and women.

name: Saul Church
description: The site where St Patrick established his first church in Ireland after landing at nearby Strangford Lough in 432 AD.
history: Saul, County Down, is where St Patrick established his first church in Ireland around 432 AD. A modern church and round tower were built on the site in 1932 to mark the 1,500th anniversary. The original church was a barn ('sabal') given to Patrick by Dichu, a local chieftain whom he converted.

name: Downpatrick Cathedral
description: The burial place of St Patrick, Ireland's patron saint, where a granite slab marks the traditional grave of the man who brought Christianity to Ireland.
history: Down Cathedral in Downpatrick, County Down, is the traditional burial place of St Patrick. A granite slab placed in 1900 marks where Ireland's patron saint is believed to be buried. The cathedral has medieval origins though substantially rebuilt. SS Brigid and Columcille are also traditionally said to be buried here.

name: Dromore Mound
description: A large Norman motte in the Down town of Dromore, one of the finest examples of a Norman earthwork in Ulster.
history: Dromore Mound in County Down is one of the largest and best-preserved Norman mottes in Ulster. The impressive earthwork was constructed during the Norman advance into Down. The town of Dromore also has a medieval high cross and cathedral.

name: Rathlin Island Ruins
description: Heritage ruins on Ireland's most northerly inhabited island, where Robert the Bruce famously watched a spider and resolved to fight on.
history: Rathlin Island off the Antrim coast is Ireland's most northerly inhabited island. It features various ruins and is famous as the place where Robert the Bruce, hiding in a cave in 1306, watched a spider repeatedly try to spin its web—inspiring him to continue his struggle for Scottish independence. The island is also an important seabird sanctuary.

name: Cushendun Caves Castle
description: Heritage features near the remarkable sea caves of Cushendun, a charming Antrim coastal village designed by Clough Williams-Ellis.
history: Cushendun in the Glens of Antrim features sea caves (used as a Game of Thrones filming location) and heritage buildings. The village was designed by architect Clough Williams-Ellis (creator of Portmeirion in Wales) in a distinctive Cornish style for the Cushendun estate.

name: Dunserverick Castle
description: Dramatic cliff-edge castle ruins on the Antrim coast near the Giant's Causeway, where the road from Tara to the sea once ended.
history: Dunserverick Castle on the Antrim coast near the Giant's Causeway stands on a dramatic cliff-top promontory. The site was occupied from the 5th century and the ruined castle dates mainly to the medieval period. It stood on the ancient route from Tara to the sea. Only fragments remain but the coastal setting is spectacular.

name: Randalstown Castle
description: Castle remains in the Antrim town of Randalstown, in the lush valley of the River Maine near Lough Neagh.
history: Randalstown Castle in County Antrim was associated with the O'Neill family. The town sits on the River Maine near Lough Neagh. The surrounding Randalstown Forest provides walking trails and wildlife habitat.

name: Massereene Castle
description: Castle remains in the Antrim town of Antrim, near the shores of Lough Neagh, Ireland's largest lake.
history: Massereene Castle in the town of Antrim was the seat of the Viscount Massereene. The castle grounds, now part of Antrim Castle Gardens, have been restored as fine heritage gardens open to the public. Antrim sits on the shores of Lough Neagh, the largest lake in Britain and Ireland.

name: Ballintoy Church
description: A charming church near the Antrim coast at Ballintoy Harbour, a Game of Thrones filming location with spectacular sea views.
history: Ballintoy on the North Antrim coast features a charming church near the famous harbour used as a filming location for Game of Thrones (the Iron Islands). The dramatic coastal scenery, including Carrick-a-Rede rope bridge nearby, makes this one of the most spectacular stretches of Irish coastline.

name: Layde Church
description: Medieval church ruins in a scenic clifftop graveyard near Cushendall in the Glens of Antrim, with views across the sea to Scotland.
history: Layde Church near Cushendall in the Glens of Antrim is a medieval church ruin in a beautiful clifftop graveyard overlooking the sea. The church dates to the 13th century and contains MacDonnell family tombs. On clear days, Scotland is visible across the narrow strait.

name: Greyabbey House
description: A Cistercian abbey ruin and associated house in the County Down village of Greyabbey, one of the finest medieval monastic remains in Ulster.
history: Greyabbey in County Down preserves the ruins of a Cistercian abbey founded in 1193 by Affreca, wife of John de Courcy, in thanks for a safe sea crossing. The abbey features one of the first examples of Gothic architecture in Ireland. The atmospheric ruins sit in landscaped grounds.

name: Ballymacpeake Castle
description: A tower house in the Derry countryside, one of the medieval fortifications in the Sperrin Mountains region.
history: Ballymacpeake Castle is a medieval tower house in County Derry near the Sperrin Mountains.

name: Inishowen Head Tower
description: A signal tower at the very tip of the Inishowen Peninsula, Ireland's most northerly point, overlooking the wild Atlantic.
history: Inishowen Head Tower is a Napoleonic-era signal tower near Malin Head on the Inishowen Peninsula, Ireland's most northerly mainland point. The tower was part of the coastal defence system. The dramatic coastal scenery and the sense of being at the edge of the world make this a compelling destination.

name: Ballyshannon Castle
description: Castle remains in the historic Donegal town of Ballyshannon, Ireland's oldest town and birthplace of Rory Gallagher.
history: Ballyshannon in County Donegal claims to be the oldest town in Ireland, with settlement dating back to the Neolithic. The town has castle remains and is famous as the birthplace of blues-rock legend Rory Gallagher (1948-1995). An annual music festival celebrates his legacy.

name: Horn Head Tower
description: A signal tower on the spectacular sea cliffs of Horn Head, one of Donegal's most dramatic headlands.
history: Horn Head signal tower stands on one of Donegal's most dramatic headlands near Dunfanaghy. The Napoleonic-era tower perches on cliffs over 200 metres high with views across the Atlantic. The headland is one of the finest coastal walks in Ireland.

name: Killydonnell Friary
description: Franciscan friary ruins near Rathmullan on the shores of Lough Swilly, in the historically rich Donegal landscape.
history: Killydonnell Friary near Rathmullan, County Donegal, was a Franciscan foundation on the shores of Lough Swilly. The atmospheric ruins stand in a quiet coastal setting.

name: Frenchpark Castle
description: Castle remains near the Roscommon village of Frenchpark, birthplace of Douglas Hyde, first President of Ireland.
history: Frenchpark in County Roscommon has castle remains and is the birthplace of Douglas Hyde (1860-1949), founder of the Gaelic League and first President of Ireland. The Douglas Hyde Interpretive Centre tells his story.

name: McDermott's Castle Island
description: A romantically ruined castle on a tiny island in Lough Key, associated with a tragic love legend and now part of one of Ireland's finest forest parks.
history: McDermott's Castle on an island in Lough Key, County Roscommon, was the seat of the MacDermot clan. The island is associated with a tragic love story of Una MacDermot, who drowned swimming to meet her lover on the island. The castle is visible from Lough Key Forest Park's treetop walkway.
